subject: ap-chemistry
title: Solubility Equilibrium: Ca(OH)₂ and Ksp
stimulus: Answer the following questions about the solubility of $Ca(OH)_2$ ($K_{sp}$ = 1.3 × 10$^{-6}$).

parts.prompt: Write a balanced chemical equation for the dissolution of $Ca(OH)_2$(s) in pure water.
parts.scoring_notes: Ca(OH)$_2$(s) ⇌ Ca$^{2+}$(aq) + 2 OH$^-$(aq). Must show correct charges, states, and coefficients.

scoring_points.description: For the correct balanced dissolution equation: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: Ca(OH)$_2$(s) ⇌ Ca$^{2+}$(aq) + 2 OH$^-$(aq). 1 point is earned for the correct balanced equation with states and the equilibrium arrow.

sample_responses.commentary: Correct dissolution equation: solid Ca(OH)₂ dissociates into one Ca²⁺ and two OH⁻ ions, shown with an equilibrium arrow.
Two errors: (1) the arrow should be an equilibrium arrow (⇌), not a single forward arrow; (2) missing the coefficient 2 before OH⁻.

alternatives.correct_example: Ca(OH)$_2$(s) ⇌ Ca$^{2+}$(aq) + 2 OH$^-$(aq). The coefficient of 2 for OH$^-$ is required because Ca(OH)$_2$ releases 2 hydroxide ions per formula unit.

wrong_examples: Ca(OH)$_2$(s) ⇌ Ca$^{2+}$(aq) + OH$^-$(aq) — missing the coefficient of 2 before OH$^-$.
Including a forward-only arrow — dissolution is an equilibrium.
common_traps: The stoichiometric coefficient for OH$^-$ is 2 (one Ca$^{2+}$ and two OH$^-$ per formula unit of Ca(OH)$_2$).

parts.prompt: Calculate the molar solubility of $Ca(OH)_2$ in 0.10 M $Ca(NO_3)_2$.
parts.scoring_notes: Common ion: Ca$^{2+}$ from Ca(NO$_3$)$_2$ suppresses dissolution. Let solubility = s. [Ca$^{2+}$] = 0.10 + s ≈ 0.10 (small s approximation); [OH$^-$] = 2s. $K_{sp} = (0.10)(2s)^2 = 0.40s^2 = 1.3 \times 10^{-6}$. $s = \sqrt{1.3 \times 10^{-6}/0.40} = \sqrt{3.25 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.8 \times 10^{-3}$ M.

scoring_points.description: For correct application of common ion effect with numerical answer: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: $K_{sp} = (0.10 + s)(2s)^2 \approx (0.10)(4s^2) = 4.0 \times 10^{-7}/s^2$… solving gives $s = \sqrt{1.3\times10^{-6}/0.40} = 1.8\times10^{-3}$ M. 1 point is earned for the correct setup with common ion and the numerical answer.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly accounts for the 0.10 M Ca²⁺ common ion from Ca(NO₃)₂ in the Ksp expression and solves for molar solubility.
Ignores the common ion Ca²⁺. In 0.10 M Ca(NO₃)₂, [Ca²⁺] starts at 0.10 M, not 0. The common ion effect dramatically reduces solubility.

wrong_examples: Ignoring the common ion — Ca$^{2+}$ from Ca(NO$_3$)$_2$ suppresses solubility; must include 0.10 M in the $K_{sp}$ expression.
Using $K_{sp} = (s)(2s)^2$ as in pure water — the initial [Ca$^{2+}$] is 0.10, not 0.
common_traps: Common ion effect: [Ca$^{2+}$]$_{initial}$ = 0.10 M from Ca(NO$_3$)$_2$. The molar solubility in the common-ion solution is much less than in pure water.

parts.prompt:
In the box below, complete a particle representation diagram that includes four water molecules with proper
orientation around the Ca$^{2+}$ ion.
parts.scoring_notes: Diagram must show four water molecules with oxygen atoms pointing toward the Ca$^{2+}$ cation. The partial negative end (O) of each water dipole is attracted to the positive cation (ion–dipole interaction). Hydrogen atoms point away from the cation.

scoring_points.description: For correct particle diagram with oxygen pointing toward Ca$^{2+}$: 1 point
scoring_points.official_rubric: Four water molecules drawn around Ca$^{2+}$ with oxygen atoms directed toward the cation (hydrogen atoms facing outward). 1 point is earned for the correct orientation of water molecules around the cation.

sample_responses.response: Four water molecules arranged symmetrically around Ca$^{2+}$, with each O atom pointing inward toward Ca$^{2+}$ and the H atoms pointing outward. (Ion–dipole interaction: partial negative O attracted to the +2 cation.)
sample_responses.commentary: Correct orientation: oxygen (partial negative) faces the positive cation, hydrogen atoms face away.

sample_responses.response: Four water molecules with hydrogen atoms pointing toward Ca$^{2+}$ and oxygen atoms pointing outward.
sample_responses.commentary: Incorrect orientation. The partial positive H end would be repelled by the positively charged Ca²⁺. The oxygen (partial negative end) must face the cation.

required_elements: Four water molecules shown around Ca$^{2+}$.
Each water molecule has its oxygen (the partial negative end) oriented toward the Ca$^{2+}$ ion.
alternatives.correct_example: Four water molecules arranged around the Ca$^{2+}$ ion with oxygen (O) of each water pointing toward Ca$^{2+}$ and hydrogen atoms facing outward. Ion–dipole interactions stabilize this hydration shell.

wrong_examples: Hydrogen atoms pointing toward Ca$^{2+}$ — the partial positive ends of water would be repelled by the positive cation.
common_traps: Cations attract the negative (O) end of water dipoles. Water around cations: O closer to cation, H farther away.
